content.problemStatement:
MoEngage gives you an array AAA of length NNN.

You are required to perform **exactly** (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) operations on the array. In one operation, you have to pick two indices iii and jjj (1≤i<j≤N)(1 \leq i < j \leq N)(1≤i<j≤N) and do the following:


Remove the elements AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​ from array.
Append ∣Ai−Aj∣|A_i - A_j|∣Ai​−Aj​∣ to the array.
Note that, after each operation, the size of the array is reduced by one. Thus, after (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) operations, only one element remains in the array.

Find the number of **distinct** values the remaining element can have after (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) operations.


Input Format

First line of the input contains TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case contains an integer NNN denoting the length of the array.
Second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers describing the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, print the number of **distinct** values the remaining element can have after (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) operations.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case starts with an integer N, the length of the array. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of distinct values that the last remaining element can have after performing exactly N‑1 operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111:  The given array is [4,8,5][ 4 , 8 , 5 ][4,8,5]. There are two possible values the remaining element can have after all the operations. These values are 111 and 777.

To get 111, we first choose i=1i = 1i=1 and j=2j=2j=2. We remove the elements 444 and 888 and append ∣4−8∣=4|4-8| = 4∣4−8∣=4 to the array. After performing the operation, A=[5,4]A =  [ 5 , 4 ]A=[5,4]. Next, we choose i=1i=1i=1 and j=2j=2j=2. After performing the operation, A=[1]A = [ 1 ]A=[1].
To get 777, we first choose i=1i = 1i=1 and j=3j=3j=3. We remove the elements 444 and 555 and append ∣4−5∣=1|4-5| = 1∣4−5∣=1 to the array. After performing the operation, A=[8,1]A =  [ 8 , 1 ]A=[8,1]. Next, we choose i=1i=1i=1 and j=2j=2j=2. After performing the operation, A=[7]A = [ 7 ]A=[7].



Test Case 222 : The given array is [1][ 1 ][1]. No operations are possible in the array. So, the only possible value of the remaining element is 111.


Test Case 333 : The given array is [2,4][ 2 , 4 ][2,4]. The only possible value of the remaining element after performing one operation is 222.

solution.approach:
During one operation we replace two numbers x and y by |x‑y|. Repeating N‑1 times leaves a single number. It can be shown that the final value equals | Σ εi·Ai | where each εi is either +1 or –1 and the sum of all εi is 0 when N is even and 1 when N is odd. Let S = Σ Ai. If we put the elements with εi = –1 into a set B, then the final value becomes |S – 2·sum(B)|. The size of B is fixed: |B| = N/2 when N is even, and |B| = (N‑1)/2 when N is odd. Therefore the problem reduces to finding all possible subset sums using exactly k = ⌊N/2⌋ elements. For each achievable sum x we obtain a candidate value |S – 2·x|. The answer is the number of distinct candidates.

We solve it with a DP that keeps, for each possible count c (0 … k), the set of sums reachable with exactly c elements. Starting with dp[0] = {0}, we iterate over the array and update the sets in decreasing order of c. After processing all numbers, we look at dp[k] and compute the distinct absolute differences. The constraints (total N ≤ 2000, Ai ≤ 2000) give a maximum total sum of 4·10⁶, which easily fits into memory using hash‑sets.

Time complexity per test case: O(N·k·average_set_size) – in practice ≤ O(N·totalSum) ≈ 8·10⁶ operations. Space complexity: O(k·totalSum) in the worst case, but with hash‑sets the actual memory stays well below the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * totalSum) per test case, where totalSum = Σ Ai ≤ 4·10⁶. With the given limits this is well within time limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(k * totalSum) in the worst case, implemented with hash‑sets, comfortably fitting into memory.
